title: Solving equations by inspection
source: Essential Mathematics for the Australian Curriculum, Year 7 (Cambridge, Greenwood et al. 2024) — Chapter 9 Equations, Section 9B. Images are direct crops of the original textbook pages.

questions.topic: classifying an equation as true or false for a given value
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): True

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: substituting the value 5 incorrectly or miscalculating the arithmetic once it is substituted
misconceptions.reason: the student must actually compute both sides of the equation after substituting 5 for the box before deciding true or false, not just guess

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: assuming any equation with a box in it is automatically 'true' because an equation is presented
misconceptions.reason: an equation is only true for the specific value substituted - it must be checked by calculation, not assumed

questions.topic: classifying an equation as true or false for a given value
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): False

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: substituting the value 5 incorrectly or miscalculating the arithmetic once it is substituted
misconceptions.reason: the student must actually compute both sides of the equation after substituting 5 for the box before deciding true or false, not just guess

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: assuming any equation with a box in it is automatically 'true' because an equation is presented
misconceptions.reason: an equation is only true for the specific value substituted - it must be checked by calculation, not assumed

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: assuming any equation with a box in it is automatically 'true' because an equation is presented
misconceptions.reason: an equation is only true for the specific value substituted - it must be checked by calculation, not assumed

questions.topic: classifying an equation as true or false for a given value
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): False

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: substituting the value 5 incorrectly or miscalculating the arithmetic once it is substituted
misconceptions.reason: the student must actually compute both sides of the equation after substituting 5 for the box before deciding true or false, not just guess

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: assuming any equation with a box in it is automatically 'true' because an equation is presented
misconceptions.reason: an equation is only true for the specific value substituted - it must be checked by calculation, not assumed

questions.topic: evaluating the LHS of an equation for a given value of the unknown
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 12

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: solving the equation for the unknown instead of just evaluating the LHS for the given value
misconceptions.reason: this question only asks for the value of the left-hand side after substituting - it is not yet asking to solve the equation

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: substituting the value into the RHS (13) instead of into the box on the LHS
misconceptions.reason: the given value replaces the box, which sits on the left-hand side of the equation, not the number after the equals sign

questions.topic: identifying the unknown pronumeral in an equation
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): x

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: naming a constant or coefficient in the equation as the pronumeral instead of the actual letter
misconceptions.reason: the unknown/pronumeral is the letter itself (e.g. x, c, b, d), not any of the numbers multiplying or added to it

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: trying to solve the equation for a numerical value instead of simply stating which letter is unknown
misconceptions.reason: this question only asks the student to identify the pronumeral by name - no solving is required yet

questions.topic: identifying the unknown pronumeral in an equation
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): c

misconceptions.reason: this question only asks the student to identify the pronumeral by name - no solving is required yet

questions.topic: solving one-step equations by inspection
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 6

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: guessing a number without checking it actually makes the equation true
misconceptions.reason: solving by inspection means trying values and checking they satisfy the equation - a guess must be verified by substitution

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: applying the inverse operation to only one side of the equation, or to the wrong side
misconceptions.reason: whatever value is found for the missing number must make BOTH sides of the equation equal when substituted back in

questions.topic: solving two-step equations by inspection
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): p = 3

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: checking only one of the two operations (e.g. the multiplication) and forgetting to check the second (e.g. the subtraction) when verifying a trial value
misconceptions.reason: a two-step equation like 2p - 1 = 5 needs the trial value substituted through BOTH operations, in order, before comparing to the right-hand side

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: not expanding the brackets before substituting, e.g. treating 4(e - 2) = 4 as if it were 4e - 2 = 4
misconceptions.reason: brackets mean everything inside is multiplied by the number out the front - the trial value must be substituted inside the brackets first, then multiplied

misconceptions.reason: brackets mean everything inside is multiplied by the number out the front - the trial value must be substituted inside the brackets first, then multiplied

questions.topic: solving equations with the unknown on both sides / with brackets, by inspection
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): x = 3

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: stopping after trying one or two whole numbers between 1 and 10 without finding one that actually works
misconceptions.reason: the question guarantees a whole-number solution between 1 and 10 exists, so the student should systematically try values in that range until the equation balances

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: treating the unknown differently on each side, e.g. for 7 + x = 2 x x substituting a value for x on the left but not updating it on the right
misconceptions.reason: when the same pronumeral appears on both sides, the same trial value must be substituted everywhere it appears

questions.topic: solving equations with the unknown on both sides / with brackets, by inspection
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): x = 7

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: stopping after trying one or two whole numbers between 1 and 10 without finding one that actually works
misconceptions.reason: the question guarantees a whole-number solution between 1 and 10 exists, so the student should systematically try values in that range until the equation balances

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: treating the unknown differently on each side, e.g. for 7 + x = 2 x x substituting a value for x on the left but not updating it on the right
misconceptions.reason: when the same pronumeral appears on both sides, the same trial value must be substituted everywhere it appears

questions.topic: solving equations with the unknown on both sides / with brackets, by inspection

misconceptions.reason: when the same pronumeral appears on both sides, the same trial value must be substituted everywhere it appears

questions.topic: translating a worded statement into an equation and solving by inspection
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 11

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: performing the described operation in the wrong direction, e.g. reading '3 less than a number is 9' as 3 - x = 9 instead of x - 3 = 9
misconceptions.reason: phrases like 'less than' and 'more than' describe what happens to the unknown number, so the number comes first and the adjustment is applied to it, not the other way around

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: giving the value of the expression rather than the original unknown number
misconceptions.reason: the question asks for the number itself (before doubling/halving/dividing), not the result stated in the sentence

questions.topic: writing an equation for a wage word problem
questions.aiNotes: Official answer (from the Cambridge answer key): 10x = 180

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: writing the equation with the operation reversed, e.g. writing 180x = 10 instead of 10x = 180
misconceptions.reason: the rate ($10 per hour) multiplies the number of hours (x) to give the total earned, so the rate must be the coefficient of x, not the total

misconceptions.wrongAnswer: solving the equation using the wrong inverse operation, e.g. multiplying instead of dividing
misconceptions.reason: to undo multiplication by 10 in 10x = 180, both sides must be divided by 10, not multiplied

questions.topic: solving a consecutive-integer word problem by inspection
questions.answer: x = 14, so 14, 15 and 16 are the numbers.

misconceptions.reason: since x + (x+1) + (x+2) simplifies to 3x + 3, the right-hand side must be a multiple of 3 for x to come out as a whole number

questions.topic: explaining why a consecutive-integer equation needs a multiple-of-3 solution
questions.answer: LHS is 3x + 3 or 3(x + 1), which will always be a multiple of 3.
